tldr: Dyte is a managed video/audio SDK with some real-time messaging primitives; Yjs is an open-source CRDT library for collaborative state. This is not a drop-in swap: you keep Dyte (or another media SDK) for A/V, and adopt Yjs only for the collaborative-data layer (shared documents, cursors, presence-as-state). Treat the move as adding Yjs alongside, then retiring any Dyte channels you were using purely for app state.
why_switch: Eliminate vendor lock-in on collaborative state — Yjs is MIT-licensed and runs anywhere
Offline-first and conflict-free merging via CRDTs, which a generic pub/sub SDK does not provide
Self-host the transport (y-websocket, y-webrtc, or any provider) instead of paying per-connection
Rich ecosystem of editor bindings (ProseMirror, TipTap, CodeMirror, Monaco, Slate, Quill)
Predictable cost: no per-connection or per-message metering on the data plane
what_to_export: Inventory of Dyte channels/rooms used for non-A/V data (presence, cursors, shared state)
Message schemas and event types currently broadcast through Dyte
Any persisted message history you want to keep (export from your own DB, not Dyte)
User/identity mapping used for participants in Dyte rooms
Permission/auth model for who can read or write each channel
Client-side code paths that subscribe, publish, or listen to Dyte SDK events
Server-side webhook handlers and access-token issuance logic
List of A/V features (video calls, recording, livestream) that must stay on Dyte
export_method: Dyte does not provide a generic 'export collaborative state' endpoint because most ephemeral state lives on clients. Audit your own application database for any data you mirrored from Dyte, and capture room/participant configs via the Dyte REST API where you store them. For collaborative editing state, there is typically nothing to export — you reconstruct it as a Yjs document on first load.
import_method: Install yjs and a provider (y-websocket is the common starting point: run y-websocket-server or host your own). Wrap each shared piece of state in a Y.Doc with Y.Map / Y.Array / Y.Text. For editors, install the relevant binding (e.g. y-prosemirror, y-codemirror.next). Seed the initial Y.Doc from your existing database on first connection, then let CRDT sync handle subsequent edits. Keep Dyte for video/audio rooms; remove only the data-channel usage you replace.

concept_mapping.target: Y.Doc (one per logical document or room)
concept_mapping.note: A Y.Doc is the unit of sync; pick a stable room/document id.

concept_mapping.target: Mutation on a Y.Map / Y.Array / Y.Text
concept_mapping.note: State diffs replace explicit messages; observers fire on change.
Awareness is ephemeral, not persisted in the Y.Doc.

concept_mapping.target: Provider-level auth (e.g. token in WebSocket URL, custom server check)
concept_mapping.note: Yjs itself has no auth; enforce on your provider/server.
Snapshot Y.Doc updates server-side to durable storage.
Combine with y-indexeddb for offline cache.
Run a Node process that subscribes and reacts to updates.

effort: 1-3 weeks for a single-document collab feature; 4-8 weeks if you need self-hosted transport, persistence, auth, and editor bindings across multiple surfaces.
gotchas: Yjs replaces the data layer only — you still need a media SDK (Dyte or alternative) for video/audio
Awareness state (cursors, selections) is ephemeral; do not store it in the Y.Doc
Y.Doc updates grow over time — plan for snapshot/compaction in long-lived documents
Auth is your responsibility: a raw y-websocket server has no access control by default
Conflict resolution is automatic but semantic: design schemas so concurrent edits produce sensible results
Garbage collection of deleted content is enabled by default; disable if you need full history

email.subject: Moving collaborative state from Dyte to Yjs
email.body:
Hey team,

We're separating our real-time stack: Dyte (or another media vendor) stays for video and audio, and we're adopting Yjs for everything that's actually shared application state — documents, cursors, presence, collaborative lists.

Why: Yjs is an open-source CRDT library, so we drop per-connection pricing on the data plane, get offline-first sync for free, and stop coupling our app state to a specific vendor's SDK. Editor integrations (ProseMirror, TipTap, CodeMirror, Monaco) are first-class.

Plan: stand up a y-websocket provider, port one feature at a time to a Y.Doc, keep Dyte rooms purely for A/V. I'll share the migration checklist and a list of channels we can retire once each feature is moved over.

Let me know if you want to review the design before we start.
